---
title: "Finite Hyperspheres"
---

Finite hyperspheres extend the suspension idea one dimension higher. The reader should treat this page as a finite scaffold: it helps organize cell counts and dimension vocabulary before the quaternion and Hopf chapters add algebraic and phase language.

::: {.warning-box}
Future widgets should keep topological claims separate from finite cell-count claims. A finite suspended-surface model is not a completed smooth `S^3` theory.
:::

## What To Notice

- S2 introduced finite sphere-like grids.
- S3 adds another suspension layer and prepares the ground for quaternion coordinates.
- The checked theorem card records the finite scaffold claim currently available.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## Checkpoint

Why should the finite hypersphere scaffold come before quaternion and Hopf material in the reader path?

:::

## Source Trail

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="S3C-T0001"></div>

<p data-paper-ref="papers/S3/PAPER_S3_01_FINITE_HYPERSPHERES.md">Paper source: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/S3/PAPER_S3_01_FINITE_HYPERSPHERES.md">S3 Finite Hyperspheres</a></p>

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
